question:
A \emph{triangulation} $\mathcal{T}$ of a polygon $P$ is a finite
collection of triangles whose union is $P$, and such that the
intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex,
or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle
in $\mathcal{T}$. Say that $\mathcal{T}$ is \emph{admissible} if every
internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure
omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $M_n$, depending only on $n$,
such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $P$ with $n$ sides
has at most $M_n$ triangles.
solution:
For an admissible triangulation $\mathcal{T}$, number the
  vertices of $P$ consecutively $v_1,\dots,v_n$, and let $a_i$ be the
  number of edges in $\mathcal{T}$ emanating from $v_i$; note that
  $a_i \geq 2$ for all $i$.

  We first claim that $a_1+\dots+a_n \leq 4n-6$. Let $V,E,F$ denote
  the number of vertices, edges, and faces in $\mathcal{T}$. By
  Euler's Formula, $(F+1)-E+V=2$ (one must add 1 to the face count for the
  region exterior to $P$). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the
  $n$ outside edges belongs to two faces; hence $F = 2E-n$. On the
  other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the $V-n$
  internal vertices is an endpoint of at least $6$ edges; hence
  $a_1+\dots+a_n+6(V-n) \leq 2E$. Combining this inequality with the
  previous two equations gives
\begin{align*}
a_1+\dots+a_n &\leq 2E+6n-6(1-F+E) \\
&= 4n-6,
\end{align*}
as claimed.

Now set $A_3 = 1$ and $A_n = A_{n-1}+2n-3$ for $n \geq 4$; we will
prove by induction on $n$ that $\mathcal{T}$ has at most $A_n$
triangles. For $n=3$, since $a_1+a_2+a_3=6$, $a_1=a_2=a_3=2$ and hence
$\mathcal{T}$ consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an $(n-1)$-gon has at
most $A_{n-1}$ triangles, and let $\mathcal{T}$ be an admissible
triangulation of an $n$-gon. If any $a_i = 2$, then we can remove the
triangle of $\mathcal{T}$ containing vertex $v_i$ to obtain an
admissible triangulation of an $(n-1)$-gon; then the number of
triangles in $\mathcal{T}$ is at most $A_{n-1}+1 < A_n$ by induction.
Otherwise, all $a_i \geq 3$. Now the average of $a_1,\dots,a_n$ is
less than $4$, and thus there are more $a_i = 3$ than $a_i \geq 5$. It
follows that there is a sequence of $k$ consecutive vertices in $P$
whose degrees are $3,4,4,\ldots,4,3$ in order, for some $k$ with
$2\leq k\leq n-1$ (possibly $k=2$, in which case there are no degree
$4$ vertices separating the degree $3$ vertices). If we remove from
$\mathcal{T}$ the $2k-1$ triangles which contain at least one of these
vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an
$(n-1)$-gon. It follows that there are at most $A_{n-1}+2k-1 \leq
A_{n-1}+2n-3 = A_n$ triangles in $\mathcal{T}$. This completes the
induction step and the proof.

\textbf{Remark:}
We can refine the bound $A_n$ somewhat.
Supposing that $a_i \geq 3$ for all
$i$, the fact that $a_1 + \cdots + a_n \leq 4n-6$ implies that there are at least
six more indices $i$ with $a_i = 3$ than with $a_i \geq 5$. Thus there exist
six sequences with degrees $3,4,\dots,4,3$, of total length at most
$n+6$. We may thus choose a sequence of length $k \leq \lfloor \frac{n}{6}
\rfloor + 1$, so we may improve the upper bound to $A_n = A_{n-1} +
2 \lfloor \frac{n}{6} \rfloor + 1$, or asymptotically
$\frac{1}{6} n^2$.

However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular
lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible,
achieves asymptotically $\frac{1}{6} n^2$ triangles.

descriptive_long.question: A \emph{triangulation} $\text{triangset}$ of a polygon $\text{polygonset}$ is a finite collection of triangles whose union is $\text{polygonset}$, and such that the intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex, or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle in $\text{triangset}$. Say that $\text{triangset}$ is \emph{admissible} if every internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $\text{boundval}$, depending only on $\text{sidescount}$, such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $\text{polygonset}$ with $\text{sidescount}$ sides has at most $\text{boundval}$ triangles.
descriptive_long.solution:
For an admissible triangulation $	ext{triangset}$, number the vertices of $	ext{polygonset}$ consecutively $	ext{vertexone},\dots,	ext{vertexn}$, and let $	ext{emanating}$ be the number of edges in $	ext{triangset}$ emanating from $	ext{vertexvar}$; note that $	ext{emanating} \ge 2$ for all $	ext{indexvar}$.

We first claim that $a_1+\dots+a_{	ext{sidescount}} \le 4	ext{sidescount}-6$. Let $	ext{vertcount},	ext{edgecount},	ext{facecount}$ denote the number of vertices, edges, and faces in $	ext{triangset}$. By Euler's Formula, $(	ext{facecount}+1)-	ext{edgecount}+	ext{vertcount}=2$ (one must add 1 to the face count for the region exterior to $	ext{polygonset}$). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the $	ext{sidescount}$ outside edges belongs to two faces; hence $	ext{facecount}=2	ext{edgecount}-	ext{sidescount}$. On the other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the $	ext{vertcount}-	ext{sidescount}$ internal vertices is an endpoint of at least $6$ edges; hence $a_1+\dots+a_{	ext{sidescount}}+6(	ext{vertcount}-	ext{sidescount}) \le 2	ext{edgecount}$. Combining this inequality with the previous two equations gives
 egin{align*}
a_1+\dots+a_{	ext{sidescount}} &\le 2	ext{edgecount}+6	ext{sidescount}-6(1-	ext{facecount}+	ext{edgecount}) \ &= 4	ext{sidescount}-6,\end{align*}
as claimed.

Now set $	ext{basetriang}=1$ and $	ext{recurtriang}=	ext{prevtriang}+2	ext{sidescount}-3$ for $	ext{sidescount}\ge4$; we will prove by induction on $	ext{sidescount}$ that $	ext{triangset}$ has at most $	ext{recurtriang}$ triangles. For $	ext{sidescount}=3$, since $a_1+a_2+a_3=6$, $a_1=a_2=a_3=2$ and hence $	ext{triangset}$ consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an $(	ext{sidescount}-1)$-gon has at most $	ext{prevtriang}$ triangles, and let $	ext{triangset}$ be an admissible triangulation of an $	ext{sidescount}$-gon. If any $	ext{emanating}=2$, then we can remove the triangle of $	ext{triangset}$ containing vertex $	ext{vertexvar}$ to obtain an admissible triangulation of an $(	ext{sidescount}-1)$-gon; then the number of triangles in $	ext{triangset}$ is at most $	ext{prevtriang}+1<	ext{recurtriang}$ by induction. Otherwise, all $a_{	ext{indexvar}}\ge3$. Now the average of $a_1,\dots,a_{	ext{sidescount}}$ is less than $4$, and thus there are more $a_{	ext{indexvar}}=3$ than $a_{	ext{indexvar}}\ge5$. It follows that there is a sequence of $	ext{seqlength}$ consecutive vertices in $	ext{polygonset}$ whose degrees are $3,4,4,\ldots,4,3$ in order, for some $	ext{seqlength}$ with $2\le	ext{seqlength}\le	ext{sidescount}-1$ (possibly $	ext{seqlength}=2$, in which case there are no degree $4$ vertices separating the degree $3$ vertices). If we remove from $	ext{triangset}$ the $2	ext{seqlength}-1$ triangles which contain at least one of these vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an $(	ext{sidescount}-1)$-gon. It follows that there are at most $	ext{prevtriang}+2	ext{seqlength}-1 \le 	ext{prevtriang}+2	ext{sidescount}-3=	ext{recurtriang}$ triangles in $	ext{triangset}$. This completes the induction step and the proof.

	extbf{Remark:} We can refine the bound $	ext{recurtriang}$ somewhat. Supposing that $a_{	ext{indexvar}}\ge3$ for all $	ext{indexvar}$, the fact that $a_1+\cdots+a_{	ext{sidescount}}\le4	ext{sidescount}-6$ implies that there are at least six more indices $	ext{indexvar}$ with $a_{	ext{indexvar}}=3$ than with $a_{	ext{indexvar}}\ge5$. Thus there exist six sequences with degrees $3,4,\dots,4,3$, of total length at most $	ext{sidescount}+6$. We may thus choose a sequence of length $	ext{seqlength}\le\lfloor rac{	ext{sidescount}}{6}floor+1$, so we may improve the upper bound to $	ext{recurtriang}=	ext{prevtriang}+2\lfloor rac{	ext{sidescount}}{6}floor+1$, or asymptotically $ rac{1}{6}	ext{sidescount}^2$.

However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible, achieves asymptotically $ rac{1}{6}	ext{sidescount}^2$ triangles.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A \emph{triangulation} $\mathcal{strawberry}$ of a polygon $dandelion$ is a finite
collection of triangles whose union is $dandelion$, and such that the
intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex,
or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle
in $\mathcal{strawberry}$. Say that $\mathcal{strawberry}$ is \emph{admissible} if every
internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure
omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $raspberry$, depending only on $lemontree$,
such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $dandelion$ with $lemontree$ sides
has at most $raspberry$ triangles.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
For an admissible triangulation $\mathcal{strawberry}$, number the
  vertices of $dandelion$ consecutively $narwhalone,\dots,narwhalnest$, and let $pomegranate$ be the
  number of edges in $\mathcal{strawberry}$ emanating from $narwhaliris$; note that
  $pomegranate \geq 2$ for all $iguanaeye$.

  We first claim that $a_1+\dots+a_{lemontree} \leq 4lemontree-6$. Let $watermelon,buttercup,canarybird$ denote
  the number of vertices, edges, and faces in $\mathcal{strawberry}$. By
  Euler's Formula, $(canarybird+1)-buttercup+watermelon=2$ (one must add 1 to the face count for the
  region exterior to $dandelion$). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the
  $lemontree$ outside edges belongs to two faces; hence $canarybird = 2buttercup-lemontree$. On the
  other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the $watermelon-lemontree$
  internal vertices is an endpoint of at least $6$ edges; hence
  $a_1+\dots+a_{lemontree}+6(watermelon-lemontree) \leq 2buttercup$. Combining this inequality with the
  previous two equations gives
\begin{align*}
a_1+\dots+a_{lemontree} &\leq 2buttercup+6lemontree-6(1-canarybird+buttercup) \\
&= 4lemontree-6,
\end{align*}
as claimed.

Now set $blackberry = 1$ and $blueberrie = blueberrie-1+2lemontree-3$ for $lemontree \geq 4$; we will
prove by induction on $lemontree$ that $\mathcal{strawberry}$ has at most $blueberrie$
triangles. For $lemontree=3$, since $a_1+a_2+a_3=6$, $a_1=a_2=a_3=2$ and hence
$\mathcal{strawberry}$ consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an $(lemontree-1)$-gon has at
most $blueberrie-1$ triangles, and let $\mathcal{strawberry}$ be an admissible
triangulation of an $lemontree$-gon. If any $pomegranate = 2$, then we can remove the
triangle of $\mathcal{strawberry}$ containing vertex $narwhaliris$ to obtain an
admissible triangulation of an $(lemontree-1)$-gon; then the number of
triangles in $\mathcal{strawberry}$ is at most $blueberrie-1+1 < blueberrie$ by induction.
Otherwise, all $pomegranate \geq 3$. Now the average of $a_1,\dots,a_{lemontree}$ is
less than $4$, and thus there are more $pomegranate = 3$ than $pomegranate \geq 5$. It
follows that there is a sequence of $platypusk$ consecutive vertices in $dandelion$
whose degrees are $3,4,4,\ldots,4,3$ in order, for some $platypusk$ with
$2\leq platypusk\leq lemontree-1$ (possibly $platypusk=2$, in which case there are no degree
$4$ vertices separating the degree $3$ vertices). If we remove from
$\mathcal{strawberry}$ the $2platypusk-1$ triangles which contain at least one of these
vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an
$(lemontree-1)$-gon. It follows that there are at most $blueberrie-1+2platypusk-1 \leq
blueberrie-1+2lemontree-3 = blueberrie$ triangles in $\mathcal{strawberry}$. This completes the
induction step and the proof.

\textbf{Remark:}
We can refine the bound $blueberrie$ somewhat.
Supposing that $pomegranate \geq 3$ for all
$iguanaeye$, the fact that $a_1 + \cdots + a_{lemontree} \leq 4lemontree-6$ implies that there are at least
six more indices $iguanaeye$ with $pomegranate = 3$ than with $pomegranate \geq 5$. Thus there exist
six sequences with degrees $3,4,\dots,4,3$, of total length at most
$lemontree+6$. We may thus choose a sequence of length $platypusk \leq \lfloor \frac{lemontree}{6}
\rfloor + 1$, so we may improve the upper bound to $blueberrie = blueberrie-1 +
2 \lfloor \frac{lemontree}{6} \rfloor + 1$, or asymptotically
$\frac{1}{6} lemontree^2$.

However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular
lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible,
achieves asymptotically $\frac{1}{6} lemontree^2$ triangles.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A \emph{triangulation} $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ of a polygon $circleregion$ is a finite
collection of triangles whose union is $circleregion$, and such that the
intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex,
or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle
in $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$. Say that $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ is \emph{admissible} if every
internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure
omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $leastbound$, depending only on $zerocount$,
such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $circleregion$ with $zerocount$ sides
has at most $leastbound$ triangles.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
For an admissible triangulation $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$, number the
vertices of $circleregion$ consecutively $outsiderone,\dots,outsiderend$, and let $gapcount$ be the
number of edges in $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ emanating from $outsiderindex$; note that
$gapcount \geq 2$ for all $allindex$.

We first claim that $a_1+\dots+a_{zerocount} \leq 4zerocount-6$. Let $emptysize,voidlinks,hollowset$ denote
the number of vertices, edges, and faces in $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$. By
Euler's Formula, $(hollowset+1)-voidlinks+emptysize=2$ (one must add 1 to the face count for the
region exterior to $circleregion$). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the
$zerocount$ outside edges belongs to two faces; hence $hollowset = 2voidlinks-zerocount$. On the
other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the $emptysize-zerocount$
internal vertices is an endpoint of at least $6$ edges; hence
$a_1+\dots+a_{zerocount}+6(emptysize-zerocount) \leq 2voidlinks$. Combining this inequality with the
previous two equations gives
\begin{align*}
a_1+\dots+a_{zerocount} &\leq 2voidlinks+6zerocount-6(1-hollowset+voidlinks) \\
&= 4zerocount-6,
\end{align*}
as claimed.

Now set $oppositeone = 1$ and $oppositeall = oppositeprev+2zerocount-3$ for $zerocount \geq 4$; we will
prove by induction on $zerocount$ that $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ has at most $oppositeall$
triangles. For $zerocount=3$, since $a_1+a_2+a_3=6$, $a_1=a_2=a_3=2$ and hence
$\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an $(zerocount-1)$-gon has at
most $oppositeprev$ triangles, and let $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ be an admissible
triangulation of an $zerocount$-gon. If any $gapcount = 2$, then we can remove the
triangle of $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ containing vertex $outsiderindex$ to obtain an
admissible triangulation of an $(zerocount-1)$-gon; then the number of
triangles in $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ is at most $oppositeprev+1 < oppositeall$ by induction.
Otherwise, all $gapcount \geq 3$. Now the average of $a_1,\dots,a_{zerocount}$ is
less than $4$, and thus there are more $gapcount = 3$ than $gapcount \geq 5$. It
follows that there is a sequence of $boundless$ consecutive vertices in $circleregion$
whose degrees are $3,4,4,\ldots,4,3$ in order, for some $boundless$ with
$2\leq boundless\leq zerocount-1$ (possibly $boundless=2$, in which case there are no degree
$4$ vertices separating the degree $3$ vertices). If we remove from
$\mathcal{chaosmesh}$ the $2boundless-1$ triangles which contain at least one of these
vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an
$(zerocount-1)$-gon. It follows that there are at most $oppositeprev+2boundless-1 \leq
oppositeprev+2zerocount-3 = oppositeall$ triangles in $\mathcal{chaosmesh}$. This completes the
induction step and the proof.

\textbf{Remark:}
We can refine the bound $oppositeall$ somewhat.
Supposing that $gapcount \geq 3$ for all
$allindex$, the fact that $a_1 + \cdots + a_{zerocount} \leq 4zerocount-6$ implies that there are at least
six more indices $allindex$ with $gapcount = 3$ than with $gapcount \geq 5$. Thus there exist
six sequences with degrees $3,4,\dots,4,3$, of total length at most
$zerocount+6$. We may thus choose a sequence of length $boundless \leq \lfloor \frac{zerocount}{6}\rfloor + 1$, so we may improve the upper bound to $oppositeall = oppositeprev +
2 \lfloor \frac{zerocount}{6} \rfloor + 1$, or asymptotically
$\frac{1}{6} zerocount^2$.

However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular
lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible,
achieves asymptotically $\frac{1}{6} zerocount^2$ triangles.

garbled_string.question:
A \emph{triangulation} $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ of a polygon $xbqvnepla$ is a finite
collection of triangles whose union is $xbqvnepla$, and such that the
intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex,
or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle
in $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$. Say that $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ is \emph{admissible} if every
internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure
omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $qorxsuvna$, depending only on $yjkdapmen$,
such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $xbqvnepla$ with $yjkdapmen$ sides
has at most $qorxsuvna$ triangles.
garbled_string.solution:
For an admissible triangulation $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$, number the
  vertices of $xbqvnepla$ consecutively $sdfweqtra,\dots,plmzbxcva$, and let $jzsorucna$ be the
  number of edges in $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ emanating from $vhqntysda$; note that
  $jzsorucna \geq 2$ for all $bercuvhqa$.

  We first claim that $a_1+\dots+a_n \leq 4yjkdapmen-6$. Let $oubxrmefa,fkyapudzs,wliqznmta$ denote
  the number of vertices, edges, and faces in $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$. By
  Euler's Formula, $(wliqznmta+1)-fkyapudzs+oubxrmefa=2$ (one must add 1 to the face count for the
  region exterior to $xbqvnepla$). Each face has three edges, and each edge but the
  $yjkdapmen$ outside edges belongs to two faces; hence $wliqznmta = 2fkyapudzs-yjkdapmen$. On the
  other hand, each edge has two endpoints, and each of the $oubxrmefa-yjkdapmen$
  internal vertices is an endpoint of at least $6$ edges; hence
  $a_1+\dots+a_n+6(oubxrmefa-yjkdapmen) \leq 2fkyapudzs$. Combining this inequality with the
  previous two equations gives
\begin{align*}
a_1+\dots+a_n &\leq 2fkyapudzs+6yjkdapmen-6(1-wliqznmta+fkyapudzs) \\
&= 4yjkdapmen-6,
\end{align*}
as claimed.

Now set $kjdhlmawe = 1$ and $wprcxtneb = gnopvlsae+2yjkdapmen-3$ for $yjkdapmen \geq 4$; we will
prove by induction on $yjkdapmen$ that $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ has at most $wprcxtneb$
triangles. For $yjkdapmen=3$, since $a_1+a_2+a_3=6$, $a_1=a_2=a_3=2$ and hence
$\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ consists of just one triangle.

Next assume that an admissible triangulation of an $(yjkdapmen-1)$-gon has at
most $gnopvlsae$ triangles, and let $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ be an admissible
triangulation of an $yjkdapmen$-gon. If any $jzsorucna = 2$, then we can remove the
triangle of $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ containing vertex $vhqntysda$ to obtain an
admissible triangulation of an $(yjkdapmen-1)$-gon; then the number of
triangles in $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ is at most $gnopvlsae+1 < wprcxtneb$ by induction.
Otherwise, all $jzsorucna \geq 3$. Now the average of $a_1,\dots,a_n$ is
less than $4$, and thus there are more $jzsorucna = 3$ than $jzsorucna \geq 5$. It
follows that there is a sequence of $gphsraecl$ consecutive vertices in $xbqvnepla$
whose degrees are $3,4,4,\ldots,4,3$ in order, for some $gphsraecl$ with
$2\leq gphsraecl\leq yjkdapmen-1$ (possibly $gphsraecl=2$, in which case there are no degree
$4$ vertices separating the degree $3$ vertices). If we remove from
$\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$ the $2gphsraecl-1$ triangles which contain at least one of these
vertices, then we are left with an admissible triangulation of an
$(yjkdapmen-1)$-gon. It follows that there are at most $gnopvlsae+2gphsraecl-1 \leq
gnopvlsae+2yjkdapmen-3 = wprcxtneb$ triangles in $\mathcal{lkjrtmosa}$. This completes the
induction step and the proof.

\textbf{Remark:}
We can refine the bound $wprcxtneb$ somewhat.
Supposing that $jzsorucna \geq 3$ for all
$bercuvhqa$, the fact that $a_1 + \cdots + a_n \leq 4yjkdapmen-6$ implies that there are at least
six more indices $bercuvhqa$ with $jzsorucna = 3$ than with $jzsorucna \geq 5$. Thus there exist
six sequences with degrees $3,4,\dots,4,3$, of total length at most
$yjkdapmen+6$. We may thus choose a sequence of length $gphsraecl \leq \lfloor \frac{yjkdapmen}{6}
\rfloor + 1$, so we may improve the upper bound to $wprcxtneb = gnopvlsae +
2 \lfloor \frac{yjkdapmen}{6} \rfloor + 1$, or asymptotically
$\frac{1}{6} yjkdapmen^2$.

However (as noted by Noam Elkies), a hexagonal swatch of a triangular
lattice, with the boundary as close to regular as possible,
achieves asymptotically $\frac{1}{6} yjkdapmen^2$ triangles.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix two non-negative integers  

  g  (orientable genus) and b \geq  1 (number of boundary components).

For 1 \leq  j \leq  b let P_j be a simple polygon with n_j \geq  3 sides, drawn pairwise disjointly in the oriented surface \Sigma _g (of genus g) in such a way that the closure  

  \Omega  := \Sigma _g \ \bigcup _{j=1}^{\,b} Int P_j  

is a compact surface whose boundary  

  \partial \Omega  = P_1 \cup \cdots \cup  P_b  

consists of the N := n_1+\cdots +n_b edges of the polygons P_j.  
A finite family T of (topological) triangles is called a triangulation of \Omega  if  

(1) \bigcup _{\Delta \in T} \Delta  = \Omega ;  
(2) any two distinct triangles intersect, if at all, only in a common vertex or a whole edge;  
(3) every boundary edge of \Omega  belongs to exactly one triangle of T.

Write F = |T|, E for the number of edges, V for the number of vertices, and V_b := N for the boundary vertices.  
Call T hyper-admissible if each interior vertex is incident with at least  

  d := 9 triangles.

Prove that every hyper-admissible triangulation of \Omega  satisfies  

  F \leq  M_{N,g,b} := \lfloor  (7N)/3 + 6b + 12g - 12 \rfloor .

(Consequently, for each fixed pair (g,b) the size of a hyper-admissible triangulation of \Omega  is bounded above by a constant depending only on N.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation and basic relations  
Let E_i and E_b (= N) be the numbers of interior and boundary edges respectively, and put V_i := V - N.

Euler characteristic.  
Because \Omega  has genus g and b boundary components,  

  \chi (\Omega ) = 2 - 2g - b.                                     (1)

Counting edges through faces.  
Every triangle contributes three edges; an interior edge is shared by two triangles whereas a boundary edge belongs to only one. Hence  

  3F = 2E_i + N \Rightarrow  E = E_i+N = (3F+N)/2.                (2)

Counting vertices with Euler.  
Using V - E + F = \chi (\Omega ) together with (2) we obtain  

 V = \chi (\Omega ) - F + E = \chi (\Omega ) - F + (3F+N)/2  
  = \chi (\Omega ) + \frac{1}{2}(F+N).                                      (3)

Step 1.  Relating boundary degrees to F  
Denote by S := \sum _{v\in \partial \Omega }deg(v) the sum of the degrees of the boundary vertices.  
Because every interior vertex has degree at least d = 9,

  2E = \Sigma _{v all}deg(v) \geq  S + dV_i.                       (4)

Substituting V_i = V - N from (3) and E from (2) yields

 S \leq  2E - d(V - N)  
 = (3F+N) - d(\chi (\Omega )+\frac{1}{2}(F+N) - N)  
 = (3 - d/2)F + (1 + d/2)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                      (5)

For d = 9 the coefficient 3 - d/2 equals -3/2, i.e. negative.

Step 2.  A crude lower bound on S  
Each boundary vertex belongs to its two boundary edges, so S \geq  2N.  
Combining this with (5) gives  

 2N \leq  (3 - d/2)F + (1 + d/2)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                  (6)

Because 3 - d/2 = -(d/2 - 3), rewrite (6) as

 (d/2 - 3)F \leq  (d/2 - 1)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                        (7)

Step 3.  Substituting \chi (\Omega ) and simplifying  
Insert d = 9 (so d/2 = 9/2) into (7):

 (3/2)F \leq  (7/2)N - 9\chi (\Omega ).   Multiply by 2:               (8)

 3F \leq  7N - 18\chi (\Omega ).

Now use \chi (\Omega ) = 2 - 2g - b from (1):

 3F \leq  7N - 18(2 - 2g - b)  
  = 7N - 36 + 36g + 18b.                                (9)

Step 4.  Final bound  
Dividing by 3 gives

 F \leq  (7N)/3 + 12g + 6b - 12.                            (10)

Because F is an integer, (10) implies

 F \leq  \lfloor  (7N)/3 + 6b + 12g - 12 \rfloor  = M_{N,g,b},

as desired. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher topological complexity.  
   The setting now involves an arbitrary orientable genus g and an arbitrary number b of boundary components.  Handling Euler characteristic χ(Ω)=2–2g–b instead of the disk’s χ=1 requires extra care and introduces new terms (the 12g and 6b contributions) in the final bound.

2. More variables.  
   The bound must depend simultaneously on N, g and b, so the entire count has to track three independent parameters instead of one.

3. Stronger local constraint.  
   The minimum interior degree is raised from 6 (original) or 8 (kernel variant) to 9, altering all incidence inequalities and making the coefficients in the counting identities sensitive to sign.

4. Deeper combinatorial algebra.  
   Balancing the negative coefficient of F obtained in (5) against a \emph{lower} bound for S is subtler than the original argument, which used an \emph{upper} bound on the boundary-degree sum.  The new direction of the inequality forces a different manipulation and more intricate algebra.

5. General formula.  
   The solution produces an explicit closed form for M_{N,g,b} valid for every combination of (g,b), not just the planar case; deriving and simplifying this universal expression demands careful bookkeeping throughout.

For these reasons the enhanced variant is substantially more technical and conceptually richer than both the original problem and the previous kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix two non-negative integers  

  g  (orientable genus) and b \geq  1 (number of boundary components).

For 1 \leq  j \leq  b let P_j be a simple polygon with n_j \geq  3 sides, drawn pairwise disjointly in the oriented surface \Sigma _g (of genus g) in such a way that the closure  

  \Omega  := \Sigma _g \ \bigcup _{j=1}^{\,b} Int P_j  

is a compact surface whose boundary  

  \partial \Omega  = P_1 \cup \cdots \cup  P_b  

consists of the N := n_1+\cdots +n_b edges of the polygons P_j.  
A finite family T of (topological) triangles is called a triangulation of \Omega  if  

(1) \bigcup _{\Delta \in T} \Delta  = \Omega ;  
(2) any two distinct triangles intersect, if at all, only in a common vertex or a whole edge;  
(3) every boundary edge of \Omega  belongs to exactly one triangle of T.

Write F = |T|, E for the number of edges, V for the number of vertices, and V_b := N for the boundary vertices.  
Call T hyper-admissible if each interior vertex is incident with at least  

  d := 9 triangles.

Prove that every hyper-admissible triangulation of \Omega  satisfies  

  F \leq  M_{N,g,b} := \lfloor  (7N)/3 + 6b + 12g - 12 \rfloor .

(Consequently, for each fixed pair (g,b) the size of a hyper-admissible triangulation of \Omega  is bounded above by a constant depending only on N.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Notation and basic relations  
Let E_i and E_b (= N) be the numbers of interior and boundary edges respectively, and put V_i := V - N.

Euler characteristic.  
Because \Omega  has genus g and b boundary components,  

  \chi (\Omega ) = 2 - 2g - b.                                     (1)

Counting edges through faces.  
Every triangle contributes three edges; an interior edge is shared by two triangles whereas a boundary edge belongs to only one. Hence  

  3F = 2E_i + N \Rightarrow  E = E_i+N = (3F+N)/2.                (2)

Counting vertices with Euler.  
Using V - E + F = \chi (\Omega ) together with (2) we obtain  

 V = \chi (\Omega ) - F + E = \chi (\Omega ) - F + (3F+N)/2  
  = \chi (\Omega ) + \frac{1}{2}(F+N).                                      (3)

Step 1.  Relating boundary degrees to F  
Denote by S := \sum _{v\in \partial \Omega }deg(v) the sum of the degrees of the boundary vertices.  
Because every interior vertex has degree at least d = 9,

  2E = \Sigma _{v all}deg(v) \geq  S + dV_i.                       (4)

Substituting V_i = V - N from (3) and E from (2) yields

 S \leq  2E - d(V - N)  
 = (3F+N) - d(\chi (\Omega )+\frac{1}{2}(F+N) - N)  
 = (3 - d/2)F + (1 + d/2)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                      (5)

For d = 9 the coefficient 3 - d/2 equals -3/2, i.e. negative.

Step 2.  A crude lower bound on S  
Each boundary vertex belongs to its two boundary edges, so S \geq  2N.  
Combining this with (5) gives  

 2N \leq  (3 - d/2)F + (1 + d/2)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                  (6)

Because 3 - d/2 = -(d/2 - 3), rewrite (6) as

 (d/2 - 3)F \leq  (d/2 - 1)N - d\chi (\Omega ).                        (7)

Step 3.  Substituting \chi (\Omega ) and simplifying  
Insert d = 9 (so d/2 = 9/2) into (7):

 (3/2)F \leq  (7/2)N - 9\chi (\Omega ).   Multiply by 2:               (8)

 3F \leq  7N - 18\chi (\Omega ).

Now use \chi (\Omega ) = 2 - 2g - b from (1):

 3F \leq  7N - 18(2 - 2g - b)  
  = 7N - 36 + 36g + 18b.                                (9)

Step 4.  Final bound  
Dividing by 3 gives

 F \leq  (7N)/3 + 12g + 6b - 12.                            (10)

Because F is an integer, (10) implies

 F \leq  \lfloor  (7N)/3 + 6b + 12g - 12 \rfloor  = M_{N,g,b},

as desired. \blacksquare

2. More variables.  
   The bound must depend simultaneously on N, g and b, so the entire count has to track three independent parameters instead of one.

3. Stronger local constraint.  
   The minimum interior degree is raised from 6 (original) or 8 (kernel variant) to 9, altering all incidence inequalities and making the coefficients in the counting identities sensitive to sign.

4. Deeper combinatorial algebra.  
   Balancing the negative coefficient of F obtained in (5) against a \emph{lower} bound for S is subtler than the original argument, which used an \emph{upper} bound on the boundary-degree sum.  The new direction of the inequality forces a different manipulation and more intricate algebra.

5. General formula.  
   The solution produces an explicit closed form for M_{N,g,b} valid for every combination of (g,b), not just the planar case; deriving and simplifying this universal expression demands careful bookkeeping throughout.
